explanation: The statement expresses that whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must hold at the same moment.

The statement 'If prop_0, then prop_1' is a conditional. In LTL, this can be represented globally as G (prop_0 -> prop_1), meaning that at all times, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at that time.

The phrase 'If prop_0, prop_1.' translates logically to 'if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 holds.' In LTL, this is interpreted globally as: whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must be true as well. We express this using the G (globally) and logical implication (->): Globally (prop_0 -> prop_1).
This statement can be interpreted as a conditional: whenever prop_0 is true, then prop_1 holds. In LTL, this is expressed globally: G(prop_0 -> prop_1).

The phrase 'If prop_0, prop_1.' means that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time (implication). In LTL, this is represented by the formula: 'Globally, it's always the case that if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 holds'.

This statement indicates that whenever 'prop_0' is true, 'prop_1' must also be true at the same time. In LTL, this is usually expressed by the globally operator, stating that at all times, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 holds as well.

The phrase 'If prop_0, prop_1.' expresses a logical implication: whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 should also be true at the same time. In LTL, the implication is captured globally for all points in time, using the formula G(prop_0 -> prop_1).
This states that whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at that point. This is a direct implication.

The statement 'If prop_0, an prop_1' (assumed to mean 'If prop_0, then prop_1') expresses that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold, globally. In LTL, this is represented as G(prop_0 -> prop_1).
This expresses that whenever 'prop_0' holds, then 'prop_1' must also hold at the same time. This is written as 'globally, if prop_0, then prop_1'.
